record_office_terminology.land_records: Register of Deeds (deeds, mortgages, liens -- one office per county)
record_office_terminology.probate: Probate Court (wills, estates, guardianships, marriage licenses -- one per county; formerly Probate Judge)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Probate Court (marriage licenses from territorial period; state vital records registration began 1911)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1911 (statewide birth and death registration; earlier vital events found in church records, cemetery records, and newspapers; some counties have earlier county-level records)

burned_county_authority: Kansas State Historical Society (KSHS), Topeka -- Kansas has several counties with record losses from Bleeding Kansas border war violence (1854-1861), courthouse fires, and the 2007 Greensburg tornado. KSHS holds territorial-era records, microfilmed county records, and manuscript collections that supplement destroyed county records. BLM GLO federal land patents provide backup for land records.
administrative_notes: Kansas Territory was organized by the Kansas-Nebraska Act of 1854 and admitted to the Union as a free state on January 29, 1861 (34th state). The territorial period (1854-1861) was marked by the 'Bleeding Kansas' border war between pro-slavery and Free State factions, resulting in significant record losses in eastern border counties. Kansas is a public land state -- original federal land transfers are documented in BLM/GLO patents. The Kansas State Historical Society (KSHS) in Topeka is the central genealogical repository, holding territorial records, state government records, microfilmed county records, newspaper collections, and extensive manuscript collections. Record offices: Register of Deeds (land records), Probate Court (wills, estates, marriages), District Court Clerk (civil/criminal cases, naturalizations). Kansas conducted state censuses in 1855, 1859, 1865, 1875, 1885, 1895, 1905, 1915, and 1925 -- all at KSHS. Native American records (Kansa, Osage, Pawnee, Cheyenne, Arapaho, Potawatomi, Kickapoo, Iowa, Sac & Fox, Wyandot) are at NARA Kansas City. German-Russian (Volga German) and Mennonite immigration from Russia/Ukraine in the 1870s-1880s is a major research theme. The Exoduster movement brought African American families from the South in the 1870s-1880s. Cattle towns (Dodge City, Abilene, Wichita, Ellsworth) have rich town and commercial records. The Dust Bowl of the 1930s caused severe population loss in western Kansas counties.

core_repositories.description: Central repository for Kansas genealogical research. Holds territorial-era records, microfilmed county records (deeds, probate, marriages), Kansas state censuses (1855-1925), newspaper collection (largest in Kansas), manuscript collections, military records (adjutant general), and maps. The Kansas Memory digital portal provides online access to selected collections. Research room open to the public in Topeka.

State library with genealogical collections including published county histories, family histories, Kansas vital records indexes, and reference materials. Provides interlibrary loan services for Kansas genealogical materials.

core_repositories.source_name: NARA -- National Archives at Kansas City
core_repositories.repository: National Archives at Kansas City, MO
core_repositories.description: Federal records for Kansas including Indian Territory and reservation records, federal court records (naturalizations, bankruptcy, criminal), military records (Fort Leavenworth, Fort Riley, Fort Hays, etc.), BLM land office records, Bureau of Indian Affairs records for Kansas tribes, and US Census originals.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including Kansas county records (deeds, probate, marriages), vital records, church records, and cemetery records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at KSHS and county offices. Key collections include Kansas marriages, probate records, and state censuses.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability guides, and links to digitized microfilm. Covers Kansas record types, territorial records, and research strategies for Bleeding Kansas, German-Russian, and Exoduster research.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Kansas newspapers. Kansas had an extensive newspaper tradition with papers in nearly every county seat. Legal notices (homestead proofs, estate settlements, divorce notices), obituaries, marriage announcements, and community news.

KSHS holds the most comprehensive collection of Kansas newspapers on microfilm, including papers from all 105 counties. Many territorial-era papers survive. Essential for obituaries, legal notices, and community history.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Kansas African American and Exoduster Records -- KSHS
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Kansas was admitted as a free state in 1861, but African American genealogical research in Kansas centers on the Exoduster movement (1870s-1880s) when thousands of formerly enslaved persons migrated from the South to Kansas. KSHS holds Exoduster migration records, African American community records, Nicodemus National Historic Site documentation, Freedmen's Bureau records (for Southern origins), AME church records, school records, and African American newspapers. The Brown v. Board of Education case (1954) originated in Topeka. US Colored Troops pension files at NARA document veterans who settled in Kansas.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Kansas Native American Records -- NARA Kansas City / KSHS
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: National Archives at Kansas City / Kansas State Historical Society, Topeka
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Kansas was home to numerous Native American peoples including the Kansa (Kaw), Osage, Pawnee, Cheyenne, Arapaho, Potawatomi (Prairie Band), Kickapoo, Iowa, Sac & Fox, Wyandot, Shawnee, Delaware, and others who were removed to Indian Territory (Oklahoma) in the 1850s-1870s. NARA Kansas City holds Bureau of Indian Affairs records, agency records, annuity rolls, allotment records, and treaty records. KSHS holds missionary records, Indian school records (Haskell Institute), and Kaw Mission records. Tribal enrollment records are held by the respective tribal nations.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Kansas Immigration and Naturalization Records -- KSHS / District Courts / NARA
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: Kansas State Historical Society / County District Courts / NARA Kansas City
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records for Kansas immigrants are primarily in County District Court records. Kansas attracted major immigrant groups: German-Russians (Volga Germans) and Mennonites from Russia/Ukraine in the 1870s-1880s, Swedes (Lindsborg area, McPherson and Saline counties), Czechs/Bohemians (north-central Kansas), Irish and German settlers throughout eastern Kansas, and later Mexican, Italian, and Eastern European immigrants drawn to mining and railroad work. KSHS holds some naturalization indexes. Bethel College Mennonite Library and Archives holds Mennonite immigration records. Fort Hays State University holds Volga German genealogical resources.

military_records.repository: Kansas State Historical Society, Topeka / NARA
military_records.description: KSHS holds Kansas adjutant general records, state militia records, and Civil War service records. Kansas contributed over 20,000 troops to the Union in the Civil War (more per capita than any other state). Multiple military forts: Fort Leavenworth (1827-present), Fort Riley (1853-present), Fort Hays (1865-1889), Fort Larned (1859-1878), Fort Dodge (1865-1882), Fort Wallace (1865-1882), and others. Indian Wars service records, Spanish-American War, and WWI/WWII draft registrations. NARA holds federal military records.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System -- Kansas
military_records.description: Searchable database of Civil War service records for Kansas soldiers. Kansas contributed over 20,000 troops to the Union army, more per capita than any other Northern state. Includes Colored Troops regiments.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records -- Kansas
land_and_property.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office
land_and_property.description: Kansas is a public land state. Federal land patents (homestead, preemption, timber culture, cash entry) document original transfers of federal land to private ownership. The BLM GLO website provides free online access to digitized patents and survey plats. Essential for tracing original settlers, especially in western Kansas where homesteading was the primary method of land acquisition.

land_and_property.source_name: Kansas County Register of Deeds Records
land_and_property.repository: County Register of Deeds offices (105 counties)
land_and_property.description: Each Kansas county has a Register of Deeds office maintaining land records (deeds, mortgages, liens). Records begin from county organization. KSHS and FamilySearch have microfilmed deed books for many counties. Eastern Kansas counties have records from the 1850s; western counties from the 1880s.

vital_records.repository: Kansas Department of Health and Environment (KDHE), Topeka / KSHS
vital_records.description: Statewide birth and death registration began in 1911. KDHE maintains vital records. KSHS holds historical vital records indexes and some original records. Marriage records are held by county Probate Courts (from territorial period). For pre-1911 vital events, researchers must use church records, cemetery records, newspapers, and Kansas state census records (which recorded births and deaths).

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Kansas Church Records -- KSHS / Denominational Archives
church_and_cemetery.repository: Kansas State Historical Society / Bethel College / Fort Hays State University / Local congregations
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are critical for Kansas genealogy, especially before statewide vital registration (1911). Major denominational archives: Bethel College Mennonite Library and Archives (North Newton) for Mennonite records; Fort Hays State University for Volga German (Catholic/Lutheran) records; Catholic diocesan archives (Kansas City, Dodge City, Salina, Wichita); Bethany College (Lindsborg) for Swedish Lutheran records. KSHS holds some transcribed church records.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Kansas. DAR chapters and county genealogical societies have published cemetery transcription volumes for many counties. Many pioneer cemeteries on the open prairie have been documented. Coverage is growing.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society publishing The Treesearcher journal. Maintains surname files, cemetery transcriptions, and Bible record collections. Hosts annual conferences and workshops.
Umbrella organization for county and regional genealogical societies across Kansas. Links to county-level societies with local collections and research assistance.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all 105 Kansas counties. Includes cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, query boards, and research guides.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Kansas had several extinct or renamed counties during the territorial and early statehood period. Breckinridge County was renamed Lyon County (1862) after the original namesake sided with the Confederacy. Calhoun County was renamed Jackson County (1859). Davis County was renamed Geary County (1889). Weller County was renamed Osage County (1859). Wise County was renamed Morris County (1859). Howard County (formed 1855) was abolished in 1875, with its territory divided into Elk and Chautauqua counties. Several western counties (Arapahoe, Sequoyah, and others) were organized on paper but never effectively settled and were absorbed into other counties. Researchers should search under both original and current county names at KSHS.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Kansas has 5 counties with significant record losses. Four are in eastern Kansas along the Missouri border, where Bleeding Kansas border war violence (1854-1861) destroyed early settlement records: Anderson (courthouse fire 1870), Bourbon (border raids and courthouse fire 1863), Douglas (Sacking of Lawrence 1856, Quantrill's Raid 1863), and Linn (Marais des Cygnes massacre 1858 and raids). Kiowa County suffered record losses from an EF5 tornado that destroyed Greensburg in 2007. Several other eastern border counties (Franklin, Miami) experienced border war disruption but retained most courthouse records. BLM GLO federal land patents provide backup for land records. KSHS in Topeka holds territorial-era records and microfilmed county records that supplement destroyed originals.

burned_jurisdictions.record_losses: Courthouse fire 1870 destroyed early deed and probate records
Border war raids (1856-1861) and courthouse fire 1863
Sacking of Lawrence (1856) and Quantrill's Raid (1863)
Marais des Cygnes massacre (1858) and border raids
